title: GaN-on-SiC vs. GaAs pHEMT: A Technology Decision Map for Radar Engineers
gist: Viettel Semiconductor (a Vietnamese fabless IP vendor) lays out a technical decision framework for radar/T-R module engineers choosing between GaN-on-SiC and GaAs pHEMT, arguing GaN wins on power density and thermal headroom for PA/switch stages while GaAs still wins on noise figure and cost at moderate power, concluding most real designs land on a mixed BOM rather than an either/or choice.

themes.theme: GaN-on-SiC advantages for power amplifier stages

insights.claim: GaN's wider bandgap and higher breakdown voltage let it operate at 28-48V rails versus GaAs's typical 5-10V, delivering substantially higher output power per millimeter of die area — a decisive advantage for PA stages in AESA T/R modules where die area multiplies across hundreds or thousands of array elements.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Quantifies the specific voltage-rail and power-density gap (28-48V vs 5-10V) driving GaN's dominance in AESA radar PA design, a technical reference point for evaluating RF front-end IP and component suppliers targeting phased-array radar.
insights.quote: GaN's wider bandgap and higher breakdown voltage let it operate at 28–48V rails instead of GaAs's typical 5–10V, and it delivers substantially higher output power per millimeter of die area at those voltages.

insights.claim: SiC substrates conduct heat considerably better than GaAs, which is one reason GaN-on-SiC has displaced GaN-on-Si in demanding RF applications, and in pulsed radar operation this thermal margin translates directly into either higher reliable output power or longer MTBF at the same power.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Explains the substrate-driven mechanism (SiC thermal conductivity) behind GaN-on-SiC's displacement of GaN-on-Si in high-duty-cycle radar, relevant for tracking substrate material demand trends in RF power semiconductors.
insights.quote: SiC substrates conduct heat considerably better than GaAs — one of the reasons GaN-on-SiC has displaced GaN-on-Si in demanding RF applications.

insights.claim: For LNA first-stage performance, mature GaAs pHEMT processes still post some of the best noise figures available, particularly at lower frequencies and power levels where GaN's power advantage provides no benefit, meaning architectures dominated by receive noise figure rather than transmit power should not default to GaN.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Pushes back against a blanket 'GaN is always better' assumption, identifying noise-figure-dominated LNA designs as a segment where GaAs retains a durable technical edge — relevant for component sourcing decisions in receive-chain design.
insights.quote: For LNA-first-stage performance, mature GaAs pHEMT processes still post some of the best noise figures available, particularly at lower frequencies and lower power levels where GaN's power advantage isn't doing any work for you.

insights.claim: GaAs foundry capacity is broader and more processes are qualified across military and space programs, so for designs under roughly 10W, GaAs's cost-per-part advantage can outweigh a GaN power-density benefit that isn't actually being used.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Establishes a concrete power threshold (~10W) below which GaAs remains the more cost-effective and process-mature choice, useful for program managers deciding process technology at the component-selection stage.
insights.quote: for designs under roughly 10W, the cost-per-part advantage of GaAs can outweigh GaN's power-density benefit that you're not actually using

themes.theme: Practical outcome: mixed BOM, not either/or

insights.claim: The article concludes that the honest architectural answer is usually a mixed bill of materials — GaN where power density and thermal margin are the binding constraint (PA stages, high-power switches), GaAs where noise figure or legacy qualification dominate (some LNA front ends, lower-power auxiliary chains) — and that treating it as an either/or choice usually means over-paying for unused GaN capability or hitting a thermal wall with GaAs.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: A vendor-neutral framework (despite being published by a GaN IP vendor) for evaluating RF front-end sourcing decisions by application segment rather than blanket technology preference — useful due-diligence lens for defense-electronics supply chain analysis.
insights.quote: The honest answer most architects arrive at is a mixed BOM: GaN where power density and thermal margin are the binding constraint (PA stages, high-power switches), GaAs where noise figure or legacy qualification dominate.

insights.claim: For X-band and Ka-band AESA radar programs specifically, where array element pitch is tight and duty cycles are aggressive, the power density and thermal case for GaN-on-SiC in the PA and switch path is usually decisive, making it the default choice for new AESA T/R module designs rather than a like-for-like GaAs replacement.
insights.speaker: Viettel Semiconductor
insights.so_what: Identifies X-band/Ka-band AESA radar as the specific application segment where GaN-on-SiC has become the default rather than a like-for-like alternative — a concrete market-sizing signal for GaN RF IP and foundry demand tied to radar modernization programs.
insights.quote: the power density and thermal case for GaN-on-SiC in the PA and switch path is usually decisive, which is why it's become the default for new AESA T/R module designs rather than a like-for-like GaAs replacement
insights.locator: Where this leaves X-band and Ka-band AESA programs specifically
